title: ‘Avatar’ Sequel Film ‘Aang: The Last Airbender’ Revives the Animated Franchise With Breathtaking Visuals: TV Review
description: It’s a shame most fans of the “Avatar” franchise won’t have the chance to see its first animated feature on the big screen. (M. Night Shyamalan’s ri
body:
It’s a shame most fans of the “Avatar” franchise won’t have the chance to see its first animated feature on the big screen. (M. Night Shyamalan’s risible 2010 live-action film will be acknowledged here and here only.) “Avatar Aang: The Last Airbender” is the first official release from Avatar Studios, the shingle set up half a decade ago — animation takes time! — to expand the cult hit animated show and its sequel series “The Legend of Korra” into a broader franchise. “Avatar Aang” does so with gorgeous visuals and thoughtful worldbuilding that would surely benefit from the theatrical distribution the movie was originally slated to receive. Instead, the movie will be available on the big screen only for a limited, awards-qualifying run in New York and L.A. Everyone else will have to settle for streaming it on Paramount+, though even a scaled-down viewing experience still delivers on both excitement and nostalgia.
	

	“Avatar Aang” takes place more than a decade after the events of the original series, meaning fans who were the title character’s age when “The Last Airbender” introduced him to the Nickelodeon demo as a 12-year-old chosen one in 2005 will find the protagonist has aged with them. (I’m one of those: my cat is named after the antihero Prince Zuko, originally voiced by Dante Basco. I had to stop myself from doing a double take every time someone addresses the character in “Avatar Aang.”) It’s a time of peace: Aang and his friends successfully brought an end to a century of conflict between Zuko’s Fire Nation, the Earth Kingdom and the Water Tribe that left his own Air Nomad culture extinct. Republic City, the future setting of “The Legend of Korra,” is newly established, complete with a Unity Tower to celebrate this new, fragile state of harmony.
	

	To quickly recap the cosmology established by series creators Michael Dante DiMartino and Bryan Konietzko, now the co-chief creative officers of Avatar Studios and producers of “Avatar Aang”: each classical element has both an associated culture and “benders” who wield their powers like martial artists. (DiMartino and Konietzko were initially involved with the Netflix live-action series, which recently aired its second season, but departed during development to focus on Avatar Studios. A new, original “Avatar” series co-created by the duo, “Seven Havens,” will premiere on Paramount+ this fall.) Water benders can freeze ice or raise currents at will; earth benders can move rock or even, in some cases, manipulate metal. Only the Avatar, a spiritual figure reincarnated with each subsequent generation, can bend all four elements at once. Per the title, Aang is the last surviving airbender after his pacifist brethren were slaughtered by a belligerent, expansionist Fire Nation. Only after 100 years of hibernation did Aang emerge from hiding and work to restore balance.

	“Avatar Aang,” which was directed by Lauren Montgomery and written by Tim Hedrick and Chrisopher Yost, bears down on that formative loss. (DiMartino and Konietzko share story credit with Hedrick and Kenneth Lin.) With the quest of the initial three seasons’ worth of story completed, Aang (now voiced by Eric Nam of K-Pop and “The Traitors” fame) has more time to ruminate on his existential loneliness. Without a mentor to guide him, Aang spends his spare time hunting for Air Nomad relics in an attempt to connect with his ancestry. On one such expedition, he runs into a violent sect of non-benders who call themselves the Denied, sending Aang on a race to recover a sacred airbender artifact before the Denied can use it for their own ends. 

	Nam isn’t the only prominent new voice actor to signal both the aging of the “Avatar” leads and the increasing popularity of the franchise, now prominent enough in its third decade to attract talent like Oscar nominee Steven Yeun, stepping in as the adult version of Zuko. Wrestler-turned-actor Dave Bautista joins the cast as Tagah, an airbender Aang discovers preserved in ice (as he once was) who promises to teach Aang more Air Nomad ways; Freida Pinto voices Sonam, a mysterious former Avatar Aang communes with for help. But well-known additions don’t overshadow the core “Avatar” crew — even as they, too, sound different now: Aang’s love interest Katara (Jessica Matten), her brother Sokka (Román Zaragoza) and blind earthbender Toph (Dionne Quan) all reunite to help with the new mission.
	

	As heartwarming as it is to follow these figures into adulthood, “Avatar” has always distinguished itself first and foremost with its style, an inventive mix of Chinese mythology, Japanese-inflected animation style and eclectic influences like the Water Tribe’s Inuit-adjacent fur robes. “Avatar Aang” ups the aesthetic ante with a succession of centerpieces: flashbacks to the Air Nomads at the peak of their powers, flooding the sky in droves against a majestic mountain backdrop; combat scenes that show a mature Aang able to bend every element with ease, or sparring against Tagah in an airbender-on-airbender matchup we’ve never seen before; dramatic, colorful journeys through a supernatural storm or into the spirit world, where Aang encounters a tusked, many-eyed creature voiced by Taika Waititi. 

	The “Avatar” franchise has not progressed forward — as opposed to creatively moribund live-action remakes, with which audiences have grown increasingly disenchanted over time — since “The Legend of Korra” ended its run in 2014. “Avatar Aang” brings that dozen-year dry spell to an end. Yes, there’s the retro appeal of reopening a closed story to bring back some familiar faces. But in telling a more mature tale about the long-term effects of Aang losing everything at such a young age, a trauma that’s left him hungry for connection to the point of ignoring Tagah’s many red flags, “Avatar” shows it’s developed alongside its fanbase. (There are also searching questions about how to heal the wounds of war that have wider resonance beyond a universe with floating buffalo.) And in delivering more stunning imagery than ever before, “Avatar Aang” extends that development across multiple fronts. It’s an evolution I’d love to see continue, even if I can’t in a theater.  

	“Avatar Aang: The Last Airbender” is now showing in select theaters and will be available to stream on Paramount+ on July 25.

schema.headline: ‘Avatar’ Sequel Film ‘Aang: The Last Airbender’ Revives the Animated Franchise With Breathtaking Visuals: TV Review

title: The Pool Films Acquires Tokyo-Based Photographer and Director Xavier Tera’s Debut, ‘The Color of the Sun,’ Ahead of Venice Premiere (EXCLUSIVE)
description: Paris-based The Pool Films has taken world rights to coming-of-age drama “The Color of the Sun,” which marks the feature film debut of Tokyo-based p
body:
Paris-based The Pool Films has taken world rights to coming-of-age drama “The Color of the Sun,” which marks the feature film debut of Tokyo-based photographer and commercials director Xavier Tera, who has collaborated with the likes of Harry Styles, Kaytranada and Rosalia.

	“Color of the Sun,” which will soon launch from the Venice Film Festival‘s Horizons section, is acted in both Japanese and English. It was shot on the Japanese island of Okinawa, which hosts more than 30 U.S. military bases.
	

	The film is the tale of a Japanese teen named Kamei and his group of friends during their final summer break before high school graduation. Their life is disrupted when they befriend Colin, a teenager raised on one of the island’s U.S. bases, “forcing Kamei to confront buried insecurities,” according to an official plot description. 


	“First sexual awakenings, blurred loyalties and betrayal unfold beneath the drone of helicopters,” the synopsis adds. “The clash and the uncanny resemblance between Kamei and Colin becomes the story’s beating heart: a raw coming-of-age film that asks what it means to belong.”

	“Color of the Sun” is shot on 35mm film and lensed by ace cinematographer Sayombhu Mukdeeprom, known for Luca Guadagnino’s “Call Me by Your Name” and “Challengers,” as well as Apichatpong Weerasethakul’s Palme d’Or winner “Uncle Boonmee Who Can Recall His Past Lives.”

	It is a co-production between Italy, Canada and Japan, produced by Janie Chartier (Twin Brains); Stella Rossa Savino (MIA Film); Antonio Miyakawa (Wise Pictures); Jeremy O. Harris and Shogen. John Lesher (“Birdman”) serves as the film’s executive producer.
	

	The “Color of the Sun” cast comprises first-time actors Naoto Shimajiri, Daido, Ichiro Andre Castro, Yuma Taira, as well as West Mulholland (“Presence,” “Animal Kingdom”).

	The Pool Films co-founder Gilles Sousa in a statement called “The Color of the Sun” a “visually captivating and emotionally resonant debut,” adding: “Xavier Tera’s unique narrative perspective and Sayombhu Mukdeeprom’s extraordinary cinematography create a truly remarkable cinematic experience.”

	Pool Films will introduce the film to international buyers immediately following its world premiere in Venice.

	Tera, who was born in Montreal and raised between the U.K., Canada, and Japan, is a prominent fashion photographer and commercials director. Besides collaborating with several global pop stars, he has worked on campaigns for brands including Nike, Adidas, Burberry and Moncler, and also veered into visual art.

	“Amid the initial waves of the Covid pandemic, I left Tokyo for Okinawa in the southern reaches ofJapan. While I had a cursory understanding of Okinawa’s recent history, the depth of its culturalrichness and historical independence really hit me,” Tera said in his “The Color of the Sun” directors’ statement.

	“The more time I spent there, the more I recognized echoes of stories I had encounteredelsewhere. Coming from Canada, a country shaped by both French and British colonialism, Ibecame increasingly aware of the parallels between Okinawa’s history and that of Indigenouscommunities around the world. Acknowledging the suffering inflicted by colonial powers, both inCanada and beyond, I found myself deeply connected to what felt like a profoundly human anduniversal story,” the director added.

	“Rather than making a film about geopolitics, I wanted to explore these ideas through somethingdeeply intimate: the relationship between two teenagers from the same place, but not from thesame background. Against the backdrop of Okinawa’s complex history, I became interested inquestions of belonging; the tension between preserving tradition and embracing change, and theunexpected beauty that can emerge when cultures collide and ultimately reshape one another,” Tera concluded.

	The 83rd Venice Film Festival will run from Sept. 2 to 12.

schema.headline: The Pool Films Acquires Tokyo-Based Photographer and Director Xavier Tera’s Debut, ‘The Color of the Sun,’ Ahead of Venice Premiere (EXCLUSIVE)

title: Rebel Wilson Defamation Case Ruling Appealed by ‘The Deb’ Star Charlotte MacInnes
description: Charlotte MacInnes has appealed her defeat in a defamation case against Rebel Wilson, challenging the findings that Wilson’s Instagram posts were no
body:
Charlotte MacInnes has appealed her defeat in a defamation case against Rebel Wilson, challenging the findings that Wilson’s Instagram posts were not defamatory and did not cause serious harm to her reputation.

	MacInnes filed her notice of appeal on Thursday, one day after Justice Elizabeth Raper dismissed her defamation and breach-of-confidence claims in the Federal Court of Australia in Sydney.


	According to reports in The Australian and Sydney’s Daily Telegraph, which cite MacInnes’ notice of appeal, she is asking the Full Court of the Federal Court to set aside Raper’s ruling and enter judgment in her favor.
	

	As an alternative, she is seeking a new trial or a determination of the remaining issues by a different judge. MacInnes is also seeking to overturn the order requiring her to pay Wilson’s legal costs.

	The appeal argues that Raper made errors in assessing the meanings conveyed by four sets of Instagram posts published between September 2024 and July 2025.

	MacInnes, the lead actor in Wilson’s feature directorial debut, “The Deb,” claimed the posts portrayed her as dishonest and suggested that she had withdrawn an account of feeling uncomfortable during an encounter with the film’s producer, Amanda Ghost, to secure professional opportunities.

	Raper found that only one of the meanings alleged by MacInnes was conveyed, and only by Wilson’s Sept. 23, 2024, Instagram post. The judge found that the post suggested MacInnes had changed her account of feeling uncomfortable about what happened with Ghost in a bathroom.

	The judge ruled that the meaning was not defamatory. She also found that MacInnes had not established that the post caused, or was likely to cause, serious harm to her reputation.
	

	As an alternative finding, Raper concluded that Wilson had established that the meaning conveyed by the post was substantially true.

	MacInnes’ appeal challenges each of those conclusions. Her lawyers argue that the judge wrongly assessed the effect of the posts, underestimated the seriousness of the meanings conveyed and erred in accepting Wilson’s substantial-truth defense.

	The appeal also disputes Raper’s assessment of Wilson’s credibility. According to the reported appeal documents, MacInnes argues that the judge did not give sufficient weight to evidence that allegedly undermined Wilson’s reliability.

	Those allegations are grounds advanced by MacInnes and have not been determined by the Full Court.

	MacInnes is also reported to have raised procedural-fairness arguments, claiming that Raper made certain factual findings without giving her an adequate opportunity to address them during the trial.

	The original dispute arose from events in September 2023, when MacInnes and Ghost went swimming at Sydney’s Bondi Beach before returning to an apartment where they were staying.

	Ghost suffered a reaction to the cold water, and the two women later entered a bath together while wearing swimwear.

	Wilson maintained that MacInnes told her the following day that the incident had made her uncomfortable and subsequently changed her account. MacInnes denied making such a complaint and said she had not felt uncomfortable.

	Raper found on the balance of probabilities that MacInnes had felt uncomfortable, had said something about the incident to Wilson and later stepped back from that position.

	The judge did not find that Ghost’s behavior was sexual or amounted to sexual harassment. Her decision stated that the court had not been asked to determine whether the incident had a sexualized dimension.

	Raper dismissed MacInnes’ case on Wednesday and ordered her to pay Wilson’s costs. Wilson subsequently described the ruling as a “substantial win.”

	The appeal does not itself set aside Raper’s judgment. Her orders remain in place unless they are stayed or overturned by the Full Court.

	Variety has reached out to Wilson’s publicist for comment.

schema.headline: Rebel Wilson Defamation Case Ruling Appealed by ‘The Deb’ Star Charlotte MacInnes

title: Chris Brown Pleads Guilty Over ‘Vicious and Unprovoked’ Attack at U.K. Nightclub
description:
Chris Brown has pleaded guilty to affray in the U.K. in relation to a “vicious and unprovoked” attack at a London nightclub in 2023.

	His co-defend
body:
Chris Brown has pleaded guilty to affray in the U.K. in relation to a “vicious and unprovoked” attack at a London nightclub in 2023.

	His co-defendant, Omololu Akinlolu, also pleaded guilty. The U.K.’s Crown Prosecution Service (similar in function to the District Attorney) described Akinlolu, a Florida rapper also known as HoodyBaby, as Brown’s vocal coach.

	Southwark Crown Court heard that the duo attacked the victim, who was not named, at the Tape London nightclub in Feb. 2023. Brown was accused of twice hitting the victim with a glass bottle to his head while Akinlolu punched him.
	

	“They continued their assault whilst the victim attempted to flee through the club,” the CPS said in a statement. “The victim was kicked while he was seated defenceless on the floor.”


	After the victim went to hospital with injuries to his head and knees a warrant was issued for Brown and Akinlolu’s arrest, although they had left the U.K. by that time. Brown was arrested in May 2025 when he returned to the U.K. as part of his world tour to perform in Manchester.

	“This was a vicious and unprovoked attack by Brown and Akinlolu in a crowded nightclub where Brown used a glass bottle as a weapon to strike the victim’s head twice,” said Claire Campbell, a senior prosecutor at CPS London South, after securing the conviction. “This kind of violence is entirely unacceptable, and the Crown Prosecution Service will work tirelessly – with the police and partners across the criminal justice system – to pursue cases like this, and prove that no one is above the law.”
	

	The BBC reported that the two men, who were present in court, denied charges for assault and grievous bodily harm as well as Brown having an offensive weapon, and these were subsequently dropped. 

	They will be sentenced in October.

schema.headline: Chris Brown Pleads Guilty Over ‘Vicious and Unprovoked’ Attack at U.K. Nightclub

title: Kino Rebelde Boards Locarno Title ‘September Afternoon’ for International Sales (EXCLUSIVE)
description: Kino Rebelde has picked up international sales rights for “September Afternoon” by writer-director Nicolaas Schmidt, which has its world premiere in
body:
Kino Rebelde has picked up international sales rights for “September Afternoon” by writer-director Nicolaas Schmidt, which has its world premiere in the Cineasti del Presente competition section at the Locarno Film Festival.

	The film is described as a work that “transforms the romantic idea of the summer retreat into a quiet meditation on contemporary resignation, fragile intimacy, and the longing for connection.”


	In the film, a couple refuses to accept that summer is over. For a last time, they head to the coast with the intention of spending two afternoons together between the sand dunes and the sea.
	

	The temperature has long been too cold for sandals and shorts; the blustery autumn wind is no substitute for cosy rays of summer sun. On the way to the beach, they cross paths with a woman they know loosely. Upon arrival, they look in vain for a dune to take shelter from the wind; a small dam is built and defended hard against the tide, an attempt to erect a windbreak is made.

	These desperate struggles against the encroaching elements are merely a brief interlude before the situation unravels into a disaster of an entirely different magnitude.

	María Vera, head of Kino Rebelde, said, “In a world where attention spans shrink to the rhythm of a TikTok video, I find no more fascinating provocation than the freedom of this film. The director opens a door to the power of simplicity, and he does so with a critical spirit, delicate humor, and brilliant sensitivity, convinced from beginning to end of our need to unlearn in order to rebuild.”
	

	Producer Maxi Haslberger of Amerikafilm said, “Nicolaas Schmidt’s work strikes us as unique with his combination of long shoots and loops. We are looking forward to sharing this unique ‘cinema of doze’ experience with the audience. It’s rather when the attention is allowed to drift that we become more receptive to what cannot be perceived in other ways.”

	The film marks Schmidt’s return to Locarno, where his mid-length film “First Time [The Time for All but Sunset – Violet]” had its world premiere in 2021 and received a Pardi di Domani Special Mention.

	His previous works include “Final Stage [The Time for All but Sunset – Bgyor],” which received a Special Jury Award at the 67th Berlinale in 2017, the First Steps Award by the German Film Academy, and the German Film Critics Award (VDFK) for best short film.

	His film “Inflorescence” premiered at Berlinale Shorts in 2020 and was selected at Vienna Shorts.

	As mentioned, “First Time [The Time for All but Sunset – Violet]” received a Special Mention at the Locarno Film Festival in 2021, as well as the Special Award of the German Short Film Award, and won best film at Festival dei Popoli in Florence, as well as at the Black Canvas Film Festival in Mexico City, Athens International Film and Video Festival, and Sicilia Queer.

	“Like Horses Standing in the Rain” premiered at the International Short Film Festival Oberhausen in 2024 with a Special Mention.

	Early next year, Schmidt will shoot his feature film “Something New Has Begun,” together with Anne Döring.

	“September Afternoon” is produced by Amerikafilm. Supported by MOIN Filmförderung and FFA.

schema.headline: Kino Rebelde Boards Locarno Title ‘September Afternoon’ for International Sales (EXCLUSIVE)

title: Edinburgh Industry Program Features Ewan McGregor, Kenneth Branagh and Bruce Dern and an Inaugural U.K. Film Conference
description: Ewan McGregor, Kenneth Branagh, Ken Burns, Christine Vachon and Bruce Dern have boarded the Edinburgh Intl. Film Festival’s 2026 industry program, a
body:
Ewan McGregor, Kenneth Branagh, Ken Burns, Christine Vachon and Bruce Dern have boarded the Edinburgh Intl. Film Festival’s 2026 industry program, a talent-heavy five-day lineup anchored by the inaugural U.K. Film Conference, billed as a state-of-the-nation summit for British independent cinema.Unspooling Aug. 14-18 at EIFF’s Festival Hub at Central Hall, the program folds starry onstage conversations into nuts-and-bolts sessions on low-budget production, private investment, literary adaptation, second-feature financing, genre filmmaking and the colonial gaze.


	Its centerpiece, the Aug. 15 U.K. Film Conference, opens with BFI CEO Ben Roberts in conversation with Picturehouse creative director Clare Binns on the future of U.K. indie film.The framing is timely. British producers are increasingly being asked to mount ambitious pics on compressed budgets, assemble public and private financing and demonstrate audience appetite earlier in the development cycle. Edinburgh’s conference looks designed as an exploration and stress test of what kinds of films the current system can still support.That question drives “Would This Get Made?,” which asks how “Whisky Galore!,” “If…,” “Trainspotting” and “Four Weddings and a Funeral” would fare if they landed on development desks in 2026.Killer Films co-founder Vachon joins Film4’s Stefanie Fahrion, BBC Film’s Alice Ojha, Altitude’s Laura Wilson and Casarotto Ramsay & Associates’ Simon Renshaw to weigh finance, release strategy and audience demand.

	The conference also takes in “Low Budget, High Ambition,” focused on producers adapting to leaner shoots, and “New Nightmares,” a genre-sector snapshot featuring actor-helmer Max Minghella and filmmakers Prano Bailey-Bond, Chee Keong Cheung and Joby Stephens.

	Vachon also fronts a standalone Aug. 14 talk, while Burns, the documentary legend behind sweeping histories of the American West, Vietnam and the American Revolution, sits down with journalist Dave Calhoun.

	McGregor follows in a BAFTA Scotland-backed conversation landing as EIFF marks the 30th anniversary of “Trainspotting,” the Danny Boyle hit which turned a fiercely local Scottish story into a global cultural moment.Aug. 15 brings Kenneth Branagh, recipient of EIFF’s inaugural Outstanding Contribution to Cinema Award, followed by “Beyond the Debut,” mounted with BIFA Springboard.The latter tackles one of the indie sectors hardest bottlenecks: converting a first feature into a durable career. Writers, directors and producers will address the long march from debut to sophomore effort, spanning development, casting, finance, sales and distribution.Monday’s “From Page to Screen” gathers Sarah Brocklehurst, Miriam Battye, Jay Parini and Marc Turtletaub from EIFF selections “Extra Geography” and “Borges and Me” for a session on literary adaptation moderated by Dr. Pasquale Iannone.

	Bruce Dern later joins Variety‘s chief film critic Guy Lodge for a career conversation spanning his remarkable work with Alfred Hitchcock, Roger Corman, Hal Ashby, Walter Hill, Quentin Tarantino and Alexander Payne.The program closes Aug. 18 with “Lights, Camera, Capital,” a Screen Scotland-backed pilot bringing angel investor Lynne Cadenhead together with producers and private investors, followed by Paul Sng’s “Unmaking the Colonial Gaze in Cinema.”“Our inaugural U.K. Film Conference is something of the jewel in our crown,” said EIFF CEO and festival director Paul Ridd.

schema.headline: Edinburgh Industry Program Features Ewan McGregor, Kenneth Branagh and Bruce Dern and an Inaugural U.K. Film Conference

title: BBC Buys War Drama ‘Kabul,’ Featuring Eric Dane in One of His Last Roles
description:
The BBC has acquired thriller series “Kabul,” starring the late Eric Dane and “Tehran’s” Shervin Alenabi, from Mediawan Rights.

	The six-part show,
body:
The BBC has acquired thriller series “Kabul,” starring the late Eric Dane and “Tehran’s” Shervin Alenabi, from Mediawan Rights.

	The six-part show, which is against the backdrop of U.S. troops’ withdrawal from Afghanistan amid the Taliban’s return to Kabul, follows the chaotic evacuation of various characters including diplomats, soldiers and civilians.

	The synopsis reads: “Kabul, August 15, 2021. As American troops withdraw, the Taliban enter the city. The Nazany family, like many civilians, is forced to flee, fearing retaliation. In this desperate and chaotic situation – shadowed by the looming threat of an attack by ISIS – French police officers, Italian diplomats, German military personnel, and American intelligence services must somehow coordinate to manage the influx of civilians. How will each of them manage to save their own lives? The countdown has begun.”
	

	Produced by France’s 24 25 Films and Cinétévé, it also stars Jonathan Zaccaï (“Le bureau des legendes”), Thibault Evrard (“The Night of the 12th”), Vassilis Kukalawi (“Kandahar”), Jeanne Goursaud (“Pax Massilia”), Gianmarco Saurino (“L’estate piu Calda”) and Valentina Cervi (“Medici: Masters of Florence”).

	The series is directed by Kasia Adamik (“No Escape”) and Olga Chajdas (“Imago”) from a screenplay by Olivier Demangel (“Atlantics,” “November”), Thomas Finkelkraut (“Thomas Finkielkraut”) and Joey Lavy (“The Shift”).

	“‘Kabul’ is a tragedy of the highest stakes,” said Nick Lee, the BBC’s head of program acquisition. “A warzone story which is propelled by the real ticking clock of the evacuation that was just five years ago this summer. Global politics remain complex, but ‘Kabul’ dramatises the biggest view of history through the smallest window of a human family.”
	

	Producers are Fabienne Servan-Schreiber and Charlotte Ortiz at Cinétévé, Matthias Weber and Thibault Gast at 24 25 Films (a Mediawan Company). Sandrine Paquot exec produces. The series was made in collaboration with the European Alliance with Mediawan Rights repping global sales.

	“Kabul” launches on the BBC’s streaming platform iPlayer on Aug. 2 and will be broadcast on BBC Four from Aug. 15.

schema.headline: BBC Buys War Drama ‘Kabul,’ Featuring Eric Dane in One of His Last Roles

title: India’s Paid Music Subscribers Could Hit 30 Million by 2028, EY-IMI Report Finds
description: India‘s paid music streaming subscriber base could grow to between 28 million and 30 million by 2028, up from an estimated 14 million as of December
body:
India‘s paid music streaming subscriber base could grow to between 28 million and 30 million by 2028, up from an estimated 14 million as of December 2025, according to the EY and the Indian Music Industry (IMI) “How India Listens, Streams and Pays for Music” report, released Friday in Mumbai. 

	The report draws on a poll of upward of 15,000 smartphone owners fielded in March and April, supplemented by psychometric research covering more than 2,200 consumers and discussions with industry executives.
	

	The numbers underline a familiar paradox for the Indian music business. Some 96% of smartphone owners consume music, and 80% of them listen for more than an hour a day. Yet just 38% have ever handed over money for music streaming, bundled subscriptions included, versus 86% who have paid for video streaming services at some point. Dedicated digital service providers (DSPs) remain the preferred destination for structured listening, used by 60% of respondents, while 32% listened to music on YouTube – though YouTube remains the single largest platform for music discovery, driven largely by short-form video.


	The report frames the shortfall as a legacy of India’s leap from physical formats straight to free, ad-supported streaming. In his foreword to the report, Blaise Fernandes, CEO of IMI, wrote that a “non-paid for music ecosystem eventually negatively impacts the creators and copyright owners,” arguing that record labels, DSPs and creators need to work together toward a tiered paid model. The report notes that India’s 14 million paid subscribers trail Brazil’s more than 30 million and the United States’ 106 million, and that China – whose music industry ranked behind India’s as recently as 2015 – has built a paid subscriber base of more than 171 million, propelling it to the No. 2 spot globally in streaming revenue.
	

	Commenting on the report in a statement, Fernandes said, “Art requires more than just inspiration; it requires economic oxygen. To take Indian music global and discover our next generation of talent, we must transition from being a passive consumer market into active patron’s market. Paying for an audio digital subscription is a direct investment by the fan to show support to their favorite performers and also helps in preserving and exporting our rich cultural heritage.”

	Vikram Mehra, chair of IMI, pointed to the survey as validation of a shift already underway among consumers. “EY-IMI’s consumer study validates our belief that Indian consumer is ready to pay for quality,” Mehra said. “We look forward to working with our partners, the DSPs, in expanding the audio paid services ecosystem in India. The eventual goal is to get India into the top 5 music market of the world.”

	Ashish Pherwani, partner and leader of the media & entertainment Sector at EY India, cast the findings as an opening rather than a setback. “Music remains one of India’s most consumed forms of digital entertainment,” Pherwani said. “The report highlights an opportunity for the industry to further strengthen subscription adoption through improved consumer awareness, differentiated offerings and innovation that responds to evolving listener preferences.”

	Composer, singer and performer Badshah, who also contributed a foreword, framed the stakes in cultural terms, writing that “the future of music will be shaped not just by how widely it is heard, but by how deeply it is valued.”

	The report’s psychometric research, conducted by MindLink, splits smartphone owners into three cohorts: Payers, who hold active paid subscriptions; Fence-sitters, who use free versions but would pay if they had no other choice; and Never-payers, who rely on YouTube and do not use paid music apps. Among Never-payers, 49% said music streaming isn’t worth paying for and 36% said it feels wrong to pay for digital products when free options exist – a sentiment also shared by 29% of Fence-sitters and 9% of Payers. Fence-sitters emerged as the likelier conversion target: 27% said paying is worthwhile once they use a service regularly, and 34% cited reliability as worth paying for. Across all three cohorts, only 7% said bundling alone would be enough to get them to value and adopt a new paid product, and 38% of Fence-sitters and 33% of Never-payers said they would rather own a collection of songs outright than subscribe to one.
	

	Several structural factors underpin the industry’s optimism about future growth. India’s smartphone base, at 584 million in 2025, could reach 735 million by 2030, according to the report, while the country’s real GDP grew 6.5% in 2025 and is projected to make India the world’s third-largest economy by 2030. The report also points to India’s expanding vehicle base – more than 400 million registered vehicles as of 2025, up from 326 million in 2020 – and its young population, with 65% of Indians under age 35, as durable drivers of music consumption. Paid subscriptions grew 37% in 2025 alone, per the report, as streamers pushed premium features and introduced more friction into free products; subscription revenues are projected to rise from roughly INR10 billion (about $111 million) in 2025 to INR22 billion (about $244 million) by 2028. Industry CEOs interviewed for the report put the market’s long-term ceiling at 50 million to 75 million paid subscriptions.

	Among those who do pay, avoiding ads was the most cited reason (44%), followed by the ability to play songs in any order (38%) and higher audio quality (36%). Among those who don’t, 42% said YouTube already gives them all the music they need for free, while 33% cited price and 30% said they simply don’t need premium features. The report’s recommendations for closing the gap include building context-aware listening features tied to activity or mood, deepening bundling with telecom, banking and e-commerce platforms, and developing multi-purpose apps that connect music with live events, karaoke and creator content – alongside continued enforcement action against piracy.

schema.headline: India’s Paid Music Subscribers Could Hit 30 Million by 2028, EY-IMI Report Finds

title: Fred Tsui Named Executive Director of Hong Kong International Film Festival Society
description: The Hong Kong International Film Festival Society has appointed Fred Tsui, founder and CEO of Moebius Entertainment, as its new executive director,
body:
The Hong Kong International Film Festival Society has appointed Fred Tsui, founder and CEO of Moebius Entertainment, as its new executive director, the organization said Friday. 

	Tsui succeeds Albert Lee and assumes the role Sept. 23, reporting to HKIFFS chair Dr. Wilfred Wong and the board.

	In the new post, Tsui will oversee the society’s full range of initiatives: its marquee event, the Hong Kong International Film Festival, along with the Hong Kong – Asia Film Financing Forum and its HAF Film Lab, the Summer International Film Festival and the HKIFF Cine Fan strand.
	

	Tsui arrives with more than 30 years of experience touching nearly every corner of the business, including distribution, financing, broadcasting, exhibition and media operations alongside international sales and co-production. That range has connected him with filmmakers, financiers, distributors, exhibitors, sales agents and festival programmers across Asia, Europe and North America.


	He currently runs Moebius Entertainment as founder and chief executive, having built the outfit into a respected international sales agency for celebrated Asian titles. Under his watch, the company has crafted global sales strategies, lined up distribution deals across major territories and steered festival campaigns for award-winning films.

	The appointment lands amid an active stretch for Moebius: the company recently boarded worldwide sales for Michelle Zhou’s debut feature “Big Little Things,” selected for the Horizons section of the Venice Film Festival, and picked up sales rights outside Taiwan to Fiona Roan Feng-i’s “Happily Ever After,” set to premiere in the Toronto Film Festival’s Special Presentations section.
	

	Before launching Moebius, Tsui spent more than two decades at Media Asia Film, eventually taking charge of the studio’s international sales and co-production division as general manager. In that post, he helped extend one of Hong Kong’s major studios onto the world stage, running its global sales operation and striking co-production deals that boosted Hong Kong cinema’s international profile.

	Earlier in his career, Tsui held senior roles at PCCW and Asia Television, gaining experience in organizational leadership, media operations, strategic thinking and content oversight.

	Outside the executive suite, Tsui is also known as a film critic and has sat on juries at festivals including those in Hong Kong, Edinburgh, Jecheon, Tallinn, Seattle, Udine, Sitges, Sonoma and Skip City in Japan. He also sits on the Hong Kong Film Development Council.

	He also belongs to the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences (AMPAS) and the Hong Kong Film Critics Society. In past years he sat on the boards of the Independent Film & Television Alliance (IFTA) in the U.S. and the MIA International Audiovisual Market in Rome. The Sitges – International Fantastic Film Festival of Catalonia has honored him with its Maria Honorifica Award.

	“We are delighted to welcome Fred to HKIFFS,” said Wong. “Fred combines strategic leadership with an exceptional understanding of the international film landscape. Throughout his career, he has built bridges between Hong Kong cinema and the global marketplace while earning the trust and respect of filmmakers, festival directors and industry leaders worldwide. Just as importantly, he approaches cinema from the perspectives of an executive, a critic, a festival juror and a lifelong cinephile. We believe he is uniquely qualified to lead HKIFFS into its next chapter, further strengthening its role as one of Asia’s most important cultural institutions and international film platforms.”

	HKIFFS operates as a nonprofit, non-governmental charity built around fostering film as art and culture, running the HAF financing forum in tandem with the festival. It launched a monthly Cine Fan repertory strand in 2013 and, in 2021, restructured its industry-facing services into HKIFF Industry, which backs non-mainstream production via co-productions, festival guidance and international distribution under the HKIFF Collection label.
	

	“It is a tremendous honor to join HKIFFS, an institution that has played such a pivotal role in the cultural life of Hong Kong and in promoting Asian cinema internationally,” said Tsui. “Having worked with film festivals throughout my career – as a sales executive, producer, juror, critic and audience member – I have seen first-hand the unique role HKIFF plays in connecting filmmakers, the industry and audiences. I look forward to working with the board, our partners and the HKIFFS team to build on the society’s remarkable legacy while broadening its international reach, nurturing new talent, deepening audience engagement and ensuring Hong Kong remains at the forefront of global film culture.”

schema.headline: Fred Tsui Named Executive Director of Hong Kong International Film Festival Society

title: Sony Pictures Sets Viral X-Inspired Short-Form Thriller ‘Los Hilos del Miedo’ at Prime Video Spain (EXCLUSIVE)
description: Sony Pictures International Productions’ Spanish operation is moving into short-form fiction with “Los Hilos del Miedo,” an eight-part psychological
body:
Sony Pictures International Productions’ Spanish operation is moving into short-form fiction with “Los Hilos del Miedo,” an eight-part psychological suspense series set to launch on Prime Video in Spain.

	Running approximately five minutes per episode, the production marks the division’s first short-form series and draws on stories by Spanish writer Salvador Gutiérrez Solís that went viral on X, captivating millions of readers across Spain and Latin America.
	

	The series builds on “El Garaje,” a mobile-first pilot that generated 28.4 million total views, more than 262,000 likes and nearly 300,000 interactions on TikTok, according to Sony Pictures.


	Inspired by the everyday-horror atmosphere of Gutiérrez Solís’ suspense stories, “Los Hilos del Miedo” is designed to translate digitally native storytelling into a premium audiovisual format while retaining the speed and immediacy of its social-media origins.

	The project also extends Sony Pictures International Productions’ collaboration with Madrid-based TAI Arts University School, integrating members of its international community of young creators into the series’ technical and artistic production.

	The partnership positions “Los Hilos del Miedo” as both a commercial production and a talent-development initiative, offering some TAI participants their first professional industry experience.“At Sony Pictures, we are always striving to challenge convention, and we are proud to be innovators in short-form entertainment and to premiere ‘Los Hilos del Miedo’ on Prime Video,” said Iván Losada, managing director of Sony Pictures Entertainment, Spain.
	

	Losada said the project demonstrates that “cinematic tension is not tied to traditional running times.”“Bringing Salvador Gutiérrez Solís’ massive viral reach on X into a five-minute episodic format allows us to bridge the gap between successful digital-native content and premium audiovisual production,” he said.

	Gutiérrez Solís, who publishes under the handle @gutisolis, described the adaptation as an unexpected leap for stories originally conceived for a social network.

	“It is especially exciting — almost unimaginable — to see these stories that I originally created for a social network end up on a platform with the global reach of Prime Video, alongside one of the most respected production companies in the audiovisual industry, Sony Pictures International Productions, Spain,” he said.

	The writer also highlighted the involvement of TAI’s young filmmakers, some of whom will gain their first professional experience on the series, and said the project could mark the beginning of a new generation of audiovisual creators.

	“Los Hilos del Miedo” will join Prime Video’s streaming catalogue in Spain at a date yet to be announced.

schema.headline: Sony Pictures Sets Viral X-Inspired Short-Form Thriller ‘Los Hilos del Miedo’ at Prime Video Spain (EXCLUSIVE)

title: Chris Brown Pleads Guilty Over 2023 Attack With Music Producer in London
description:
Chris Brown
                          Pleads Guilty
                          Over 2023 London Fi
body:
Chris Brown
                          Pleads Guilty
                          Over 2023 London Fight
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 24, 2026
                            5:56 AM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Chris Brown was just convicted in a court of law ... after pleading guilty to attacking a music producer in a London nightclub three years ago, according to Reuters.
      
    
            The rapper and his co-defendant, Omololu Akinlolu, appeared Friday in London's Southwark Crown ​Court, where they copped to a charge of affray relating to an attack on Abraham Diaw in London's Tape ​nightclub in February 2023.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            The two admitted they "used or ‌threatened unlawful violence towards another and their conduct taken together was such as would cause a person of reasonable firmness ... ​to fear ​for their ⁠personal safety."
      

            Prosecutors agreed to dismiss the more serious charge of attempting to inflict grievous bodily harm and a lesser charge of assault causing actual bodily harm. 
      
    
            Brown and ​Akinlolu are set to be sentenced on October 26.

title: Jay-Z Fighting Grandma of Man Claiming to Be His Son Over 6-Figure Debt
description:
Jay-Z
                          Grandma of Man Claiming to Be My Kid Betta Have My Money
body:
Jay-Z
                          Grandma of Man Claiming to Be My Kid Betta Have My Money
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 24, 2026
                            1:00 AM PDT
                                                                      |
                                                                Updated 
                                  July 24, 2026
                                6:16 AM PDT
                                    
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Jay-Z is going after the grandmother of the man who claims he is the rapper's son … and the music mogul wants to make sure the woman can't dodge a 6-figure sum she owes him.
      
    
            According to court docs, obtained by TMZ, Jay-Z filed legal docs related to Lillie Coley’s ongoing bankruptcy case.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Jay-Z is asking that the $119K that Lillie was ordered to pay him not be wiped clean as part of her bankruptcy. He said the debt can’t be erased because the judge awarded it due to Lillie’s “fraudulent statements and misrepresentations designed to harass and target” him.
      

            The musician’s lawyer says Lillie, for the past 10 years, has “harassed” him through “numerous unfounded court filings, online harassment campaigns and public statements.”
      
    
            As you may recall, Lillie’s grandson is Rymir Satterthwaite, who has claimed Jay-Z is his father for years. Jay-Z fought back against those claims in court.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            In his suit, Jay-Z claims he has spent hundreds of thousands in legal fees fighting Lillie and Rymir. He pointed out every lawsuit brought against him by Lillie has been dismissed … but he says the pattern of harassment has continued, and he wants to make sure she can't escape from the debt she now owes him.
      
    
            Lillie asked the court to throw out Jay-Z’s petition, claiming he's failed to meet various procedural requirements. The case is ongoing.
      
    
            Rymir claims his mom, Wanda, hooked up with Jay-Z, which led to his birth. His mother is now dead.

title: Odysseus in 'The Odyssey' 'Memba Him?!
body:

                                                                      |

            American actor Armand Assante was 47 years old when he played Odysseus -- one of the most famous heroes of Greek mythology -- the brilliant warrior king carrying the weight of his past -- in the 1997 miniseries "The Odyssey."
      
    
            Armand shared the big screen with Vanessa Williams, Greta Scacchi, and Isabella Rossellini.
      
    
            Guess what he looks like now at 76 years old!

title: NBA Star Anthony Edwards Marries Longtime GF Shannon Jackson
description:
NBA Star Anthony Edwards
                          Marries Longtime GF Shannon Jackson
body:
NBA Star Anthony Edwards
                          Marries Longtime GF Shannon Jackson
                      
      
    
            NBA star Anthony Edwards is officially off the market ... the Minnesota Timberwolves superstar married his longtime girlfriend, Shannon Jackson ... TMZ has confirmed.
      
    
            Minnesota marriage records show Edwards and Jackson tied the knot on July 16, after first getting together in 2020. The couple shares a 2-year-old daughter, Aislynn.
      
    
            As TMZ previously reported, Anthony recently scored a big win in his court battle with his ex, Ayesha Howard, over their 1-year-old daughter. 
      
    
            Story developing ...
